caravan.text:
VEHICLE — CARAVAN / RV / TOWING:
• Height/width: watch for low bridges, narrow towns (old gold rush streets), some national park entry gates (2.1m limit common). Check before you commit.
• Mountain passes: avoid Great Alpine Road (VIC) and Gillies Highway (QLD) with a heavy van. Steep grades and hairpins are brutal when towing.
• Fuel range drops 30–40% when towing. Plan fuel stops more frequently. Top up whenever you see a servo — never wait for the low fuel light.
• Dump points: most large fuel stations and caravan parks have them. Free to $10. The CMCA dump point directory (cmca.net.au) is the best resource.
• Free camps with dump points + water are gold. WikiCamps and Camps Australia Wide apps are essential for RV travellers. Many free camps have 72hr stay limits.
• Gravel roads: drop to 80km/h or less. Stones destroy tyres, windscreens, and anything hanging low under the van. Pull completely left when others pass.
• Size limits at national park campgrounds — check NP websites before arriving.

4wd.text:
VEHICLE — 4WD / OFF-ROAD:
• Access: you can reach unsealed tracks, station stays, gorges, and remote camps that sedans simply can’t. This significantly expands what’s possible.
• Tyre pressures: lower for sand (20–25 PSI), higher for rocky tracks (30–32 PSI). Carry a portable air compressor to reinflate on the way out.
• River crossings: walk it first. Check depth (thigh-height max for most 4WDs), current strength, and entry/exit points. Cross in low range, keep steady momentum, don’t stop in the water. One vehicle at a time.
• Recovery gear essentials: MaxTrax boards, snatch strap, shackles, tree trunk protector, hi-lift jack, air compressor. Know how to use them before you need to.
• Remote fuel: carry jerry cans for legs over 400km between bowsers. Many remote stations are card-only or cash-only — carry both.
• Sat comms: InReach, SPOT, or PLB mandatory for any Cape York, Kimberley, SA/NT outback travel. Tell someone your route and expected return.

standard.text:
VEHICLE — STANDARD CAR:
• Stick to sealed roads unless a specific unsealed road is rated suitable for 2WD. Some national park access roads are unsealed but graded (manageable in dry weather).
• Fuel planning: allow 600–800km as your maximum between fills as a safety margin. In remote areas, plan to fill at every town regardless of tank level.
• Ground clearance limits: some creek crossings and station tracks aren’t suitable even when dry. Ask locals or check WikiCamps reviews.
• If you break down remotely: stay with the vehicle, it’s visible from the air. Call 000 if in range, or use EPIRB/PLB if you have one. Wait for help.
